story:
My stomach is a sailor’s knot as I wait for Jake: thirty-two, Leo, profuse surfer, full-time plumber, occasional trouble—at least according to the blind dating app that believes chemistry trumps photos. I used to think I knew what love was—teenage butterflies, flirty messages, someone to hold your hand. Now, as Sarah: thirty-three, Aries, profuse dreamer, full-time nurse, occasionally depressed, love feels like a vacancy in my chest the world can’t fill. The clock isn’t helping; apparently, if I don’t fall in love and have babies soon, my pregnancies will be classified as geriatric. Geriatric.

So I’m here, candlelit table, napkin fidgeting, heart galloping like a racehorse, counting down the seconds until Jake is officially late. I’d planned for traffic, no parking, sudden storms, even aliens. Nothing would surprise me in dating. Being late, though? Bad sign.

Then the astrological gods deliver: a golden-haired man appears at the front, the kind who could be a Leo without trying. “Hi, I’m here for dinner. The booking is under Sarah,” he tells the waitress. My chest lifts. A punctual man! I raise my hand, straighten my spine, paste on a normal-not-manic smile. I already know the real Jake will arrive late—shirt wrinkled, eyes a little bloodshot, a lion’s head tattoo roaring across his neck—but I keep smiling.

He comes over—crisp collared shirt tucked into dark trousers, belt buckle catching candlelight, cheeks slightly flushed like he rushed or blushed, eyes sure and bright. “Sarah?” he asks. “Lovely to meet you,” I manage. He leans in for a brief, warm hug—linen and cologne—then sits, apologizing for being held up by an emergency at work. “It was touch and go, but they pulled through,” he says.

Touch and go? Who almost doesn’t pull through during plumbing? I don’t get long to puzzle it out; he leans forward, eyes softening. “Enough about me—how was your day?”

A unicorn. In this city. On a date. I tell him one of my long-term patients finally got cleared for discharge. He grins, suggestion sparkling. “That’s great. Shall we celebrate with champagne?” He orders a bottle that sounds fancy enough to need a translator. Conversation flows. The champagne does, too. My gaze drifts: two buttons undone at his chest, ecru linen, a Rolex winking at his wrist.

A Rolex. For a plumber? I’ve dated tradies. Tradies don’t tend to drink champagne or wear watches worth half a year’s pay. Then he asks, casual as a heartbeat, “So, how long have you been at Mercy Private Hospital?”

“Mercy Private?” The axis of my world tilts. “You were just telling me about it last night,” he adds.

Last night? We didn’t talk last night.

“Sorry, Jake, I think I’m—”

“Who’s Jake?” he says.

Dread sluices through me. “I’m Jake,” another man declares, arriving with a plod, shirt wrinkled, eyes a little bloodshot, a lion’s head tattoo roaring across his neck.

Jake: thirty-two, Leo. Profuse surfer, full-time plumber, occasional trouble.

The first man blinks. I blink back. The booking was under Sarah; he heard “Sarah” and found me. He is not Jake, probably not a plumber, very possibly the kind of man who says “touch and go” for patients and wears a Rolex to dinner. The real Jake—late, rumpled, exactly as advertised—has finally arrived.

The app worked perfectly. Just not in the way I expected.
